Small angular brown lesions appear on leaves, mostly adjacent to veins, which later become greyish white centre with dark brown or reddish margin. The lesions may be seen on the petioles and stem. Minute water-soaked lesion appears on the pods initially and becomes brown and enlarges to form circular, spot with dark centre with bright red or yellow margin. The infected pods have discoloured seeds. To control this disease, treat the seeds with Carbendazim or Mancozeb@ 2 gram per kg seeds and using foliar spray with Carbendazim 12%+Mancozeb 63% WP @400 gram or Copper Oxychloride 50% WG @600gram or Tebuconazole 25.9% EC @ 300 gram or Kasugamycin 3% SL @300 gram /200 lit of water per acre.

Let’s discuss about the Rust disease management of Blackgram. The disease appears as circular reddish-brown spots which appear more commonly on the lower portion of the leaves. When leaves are severely infected, both surfaces are fully covered by rust pustules and shrivelling followed by defoliation resulting in yield losses. To control this disease, treat the seeds with Carbendazim or Mancozeb @2 gram per kg seeds. Dusting with sulphur 85% DP @6-8kg or doing foliar spray with the mixture medicines of Captan 70% +Hexaconazole 5% WP @ 0.6 gram or Difenoconazole 25% EC @0.5 ml/lit per acre.

Today we will discuss about management of Root rot in Blackgram crop. When the soil is wet, the tip of the plant rots, the roots get rot just near the soil, and finally the plant dies completely. To manage root rot disease, drenching of root zone and soil should be done with Validamycin 3 % L @ 400-ml/acre. To spray the fungicide 200-litre of water per acre is required.

Let’s discuss about the Bacterial leaf blight disease management of Blackgram. The fungus attacks all aerial part parts and at any stage of plant growth. Symptoms are circular, black, sunken spots with dark center and bright red orange margins on leaves and pods. In severe infections, the affected parts wither off. Seedlings get blighted due to infection soon after seed germination. To control this disease, treat the seeds with carbendazim/ Mancozeb or @ 2gram/kg seeds and using foliar spray with Streptocyclin (Streptomycin sulphate 90% + Tetracyclin hydrochloride 10%) SP@30 gram+ Copper oxychloride 50% WP @ 500g or (Streptomycin sulphate 9%+ Tetracyclin hydrochloride 1%) + Copper oxychloride 50% WP @ 300gram + 500gram /200 ltr of water per acre.

Let’s discuss about the Wilt disease management of Blackgram. The crop may be affected by disease at any stage. Dead seedlings or matured plants, typically in patches, are the field symptoms of wilt. At the seedlings stage, after 3-5 weeks of sowing. Whole seedlings collapse and lying flat and on the ground with dull green leaves and a shrunken stem petioles and leaflets drop off at the adult stage, followed by the entire plants.  To control this disease deep summer ploughing should be done, always use disease free seeds and avoid sowing when temperatures are high. Treat the seeds with Tebuconazole 2% DS@ 2gram/kg of seed and drench the soil with Metiram 75% WG@ 300 gram with 200 ltr of water per acre.

Today we will discuss about pod borer management in Blackgram. The larvae of this insect first feed on the young leaves, then eat the growing pods or fruits by piercing the seeds. When eating the seeds, the insect head stays inside pod and half of its body is seen hanging outside.  To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 3000 PPM) @ 600-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. After 7-10 days, spray Bacillus thuringenesis @ 400 g/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. For chemical control, spray Chlorpyriphos 50 % + Cypermethrin 5 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Profenophos 50 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Emamectin benzoate 5 % SG @ 90 g/acre or Chlorantraniliprole 18.5 % SC @80-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. Thank You.

When Blackgram in early growing stage wilted in patches, which is caused by stem fly. These wilting symptoms seen during fungal attack. But if you tear open the stem of the wilted plant and see a white, headless, thin, rice-like worm inside, then it is due to the stem fly and if it is not there, then it is fungal. To overcome this problem, sow 10% more seeds at the time of sowing so that even if some plants are lost, the number of plants per acre will remain the same and the yield will not be reduced. Apart from this, treat the seeds with the insecticide with Thiamethoxam 70% WS @ 5 gram or Imidachloprid 70% WS @ 5 gram or Thiamethoxam 30% FS @ 10 ml or Imidachloprid 48 % FS @ 10 ml and sow them. However, if the attack occurs, spray the affected crop with 40 grams of Thiamethoxam 25% WG mixed in 200 liters of water per acre.

The maggots bore into developing pods and feed on maturing seeds, causing significant yield and quality losses. Infestation is more severe during humid and cloudy weather. Remove and destroy affected pods during field inspection. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM) @ 600-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. You can also use Trichogramma chilonis card @ (1-2)/acre (100000/ha) at weekly interval for 6 times by splitting each card into 12 pieces at the scheduled points. For chemical control, spray Chlorpyriphos 50 % + Cypermethrin 5 % E.C @ 200-ml/acre or Lambda Cyhalothrin 5% EC @ 300 ml/acre or Profenophos + Cypermethrin @ 200 ml/acre or Novaluron 10% EC @ 200 ml/acre during afternoon hours by mixing with 200 litres of water.

Today we will discuss about the management of Bihar hairy caterpillar in Blackgram   cultivation. The caterpillars attack the young growing leaves, shoots in early stage and later they eat entire plant, causing the plant to appear stunted. Catch and kill the Bihar hairy caterpillar's butterfly by setting up a light traps @ 2/acre. Use Tricho cards (50,000- 100000 eggs) @ 2/acre. When there is dew in the morning dusting with Chloropyriphos 1.5% dust @ 10 kg per acre or spray with Profenophos @ 400 ml/acre or Profenophos + Cypermethrin @ 200 ml or Emamectin benzoate @ 80 g/acre in 200 liters of water.

Today we will discuss about Aphid management in Blackgram. It sucks the sap from the stems, leaves, tips and pod, causing the plant to become weak, the leaves to turn yellow and the deformed seeds. This insect is a carrier of the mosaic viral disease. As a result, the leaves to turn yellow and curl. Flowering is delayed. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @ 600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. At primary stage of infestation, spray Verticillium lecani @ 5 g/lit of water. Along with this broadcast 5000 no. of eggs of Chrysoperla carnea per acre by mixing with saw dust. Try to conserve other parasitoids in field like Coccinellid beetle and syrphid fly. To manage Aphids chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 60 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre by mixing in 200 litres of water. Thank You.

Today we will discuss about Whitefly management in Blackgram. Because of sucking the sap from the leaves, the leaves turn yellow, curl and dry up and the plant becomes weak. It is a carrier of yellow vein mosaic disease. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @ 600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. At primary stage of infestation, spray Verticillium lecani @ 5 g/lit of water. Along with this broadcast 5000 no. of eggs of Chrysoperla carnea per acre by mixing with saw dust. Try to conserve other parasitoids in field like Coccinellid beetle and syrphid fly. To manage Whitefly chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 60 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre by mixing in 200 litres of water. Thank You.

Today we will discuss about Jassid management in Blackgram. Nymphs suck the sap and because of continuous sucking of the sap from the leaves, the leaves turn yellow, curled and fall off and the plant becomes weak. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. To manage Jassids chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25 % WG @ 50-gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20 % SP @ 50-gram/acre or Fenobucarb @ 400-ml/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Harvesting should be done minimum 1 week after insecticide spraying. Thank You.

Today we will discuss about Thrips management in Blackgram. Very small, tiny insects suck the sap from leaves, flowers and stems, causing the leaves to curl. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @ 600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Blue sticky traps per acre. At primary stage of infestation, spray Verticillium lecani @ 5 g/lit of water. Along with this broadcast 5000 no. of eggs of Chrysoperla carnea per acre by mixing with saw dust. Try to conserve other parasitoids in field like Coccinellid beetle and syrphid fly. To manage Thrips chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 60 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre by mixing in 200 litres of water. Thank You.

Today we will discuss about Tobacco Caterpillar management in Blackgram. Freshly hatched larvae are active in the evening and at night, feed gregariously by scraping the chlorophyll content of leaves. soon disperse, hiding usually in the soil around the base of the plants during the day. Later stage caterpillar feed voraciously so that only petioles and branches are left behind. Caterpillar bores into the groundnut pods and feed on them. Caterpillars also show migration from one plot to another in search of food. To manage this pest during primary stages of pest infestation proper surveillance should be done to collect and destroy egg mass and caterpillar of the pest. Monitor the emergence of adult moths by setting up Pheromone traps having Spodo lure @ 6-8 nos/acre. Check the migration of caterpillars by dusting with Chlorpyriphos 1.5 % D @ 10 kg around the field. For chemical control spray Novaluron 5.25 %+ Indoxacarb 4.25 % @ 400-ml/acre or Lambda Cyhalothrin 4.9 CS @ 300 ml or Flubendiamide 20 % WG  @ 120 gram. For spraying of insecticides 200 litre of water per acre is required. Thank You.

When Arhar in early growing stage wilted in patches, which is caused by stem fly. These wilting symptoms seen during fungal attack. If you find stem swelling thickening cracking of stem or rusty red coloured at the base young plant leaves turn yellow and drooping followed by stunted growth and wilting. But if you tear open the stem of the wilted plant and see a yellowish white, headless, rice-like maggot inside, then it is due to the stem fly and if it is not there, then it is fungal. To overcome this problem, sow 10% more seeds at the time of sowing so that even if some plants are lost, the number of plants per acre will remain the same and the yield will not be reduced. Apart from this, treat the seeds with the insecticide with Thiamethoxam 70% WS @ 5 gram or Imidachloprid 70% WS @ 5 gram or Thiamethoxam 30% FS @ 10 ml or Imidachloprid 48 % FS @ 10 ml and sow them. However, if the attack occurs, spray the affected crop with 40 grams of Thiamethoxam 25% WG or Fipronil 50% SC @ 100 ml in 200 liters of water per acre.

Today we will discuss about Leaf hopper management in Arhar. Nymphs suck the sap and because of continuous sucking of the sap from the leaves, the leaves turn yellow, curled and fall off and the plant becomes weak. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. To manage Jassids chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25 % WG @ 50-gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20 % SP @ 50-gram/acre or Fenobucarb @ 400-ml/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Harvesting should be done minimum 1 week after insecticide spraying.

Today we will discuss about Aphid management in Arhar. It sucks the sap from the stems, leaves, tips and pod, causing young leaves to become twisted and curl inward or downward then the plant to become weak, the leaves to turn yellow, growth stunted and the deformed seeds. Aphids excrete honeydew sooty mould growth in leaves affect photosynthesis. It acts as vectors transmitting viruses’ diseases like mosaic. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @ 600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. Broadcast 5000 no. of eggs of Chrysoperla carnea per acre by mixing with saw dust. Try to conserve other parasitoids in field like Coccinellid beetle and syrphid fly. To manage Aphids chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 60 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Dinotefuran 20 % SG @ 80 g/acre or Flonicamid 50 % WG @ 60- gram/acre or Pymetrozine 50% WG @ 120 g/acre by mixing in 200 litres of water. Thank You.

Today we will discuss about Thrips management in Arhar. Very small, tiny insects suck the sap from leaves, flowers and stems, due to its continuous sucking the silvery streaks or patches on leaves. The leaves turn yellow become distorted rolled cupped inward especially from tip. The growing point damaged and leaves curl. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @ 600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Blue sticky traps per acre. At primary stage of infestation, spray Verticillium lecani @ 5 g/lit of water. Along with this broadcast 5000 no. of eggs of Chrysoperla carnea per acre by mixing with saw dust. Try to conserve other parasitoids in field like Coccinellid beetle and syrphid fly. To manage Thrips chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 60 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Dinotefuran 20 % SG @ 80 g/acre or Flonicamid 50 % WG @ 60- gram/acre or Pymetrozine 50% WG @ 120 g/acre by mixing in 200 litres of water.

Today we will discuss about Whitefly management in Arhar. The nymps and adult suck sap from tender leaves, from under surface due to continuous sucking leaves turn yellow, curl inward. Feeding reduce plant vitality, leading to yellowing, drooping and premature leaf drop. Heavy infestation leads to stunted growth of plant. Whitefly secrets honeydew, which gives rise to sooty mould growth on leaf surfaces, it will reduce photosynthesis. It transmit viral disease like YVMV so it is very harmful as it is act as a vector of virus disease. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @ 600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. At primary stage of infestation, spray Verticillium lecani @ 5 g/lit of water. Along with this broadcast 5000 no. of eggs of Chrysoperla carnea per acre by mixing with saw dust. Try to conserve other parasitoids in field like Coccinellid beetle and syrphid fly. To manage Whitefly chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 60 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Dinotefuran 20 % SG @ 80 g/acre or Flonicamid 50 % WG @ 60- gram/acre or Pymetrozine 50% WG @ 120 g/acre or Thiamethoxam 8.8% + Chlorantraniliprole 17.5% SC @ 50 ml by mixing in 200 litres of water.

Today we will discuss about pod borer management in Arhar. The larvae of this insect first feed on the young leaves, flower buds and flowers then eat the growing pods by piercing the seeds. When eating the seeds, the insect head stays inside pod and half of its body is seen hanging outside. Infested flowers and pods are often tied together by web like frass. Avoid very close plant spacing. Install bird perches. Install Pheromone trap with Heli lure @ 6-8/acre.  To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 3000 PPM) @ 600-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. After 7-10 days, spray Bacillus thuringenesis @ 400 g/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. For chemical control, spray Chlorpyriphos 50 % + Cypermethrin 5 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Profenophos 50 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Emamectin benzoate 5 % SG @ 90 g/acre or Chlorantraniliprole 18.5 % SC @80-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water.

Today we’ll discuss about the Powdery mildew management of Arhar. Due to this disease, small patches of white powder coating initially develop on both surfaces of older leaves. The superficial growth ultimately covers the entire leaf area. The diseased areas turn brown and dry leading to premature defoliation and death. Fruits remain underdeveloped and are deformed. The disease also creates forced maturity of the infected plants which results in heavy yield losses. Treat the seeds with Carbendazim 50%WP @ 2 gram/ kg of seeds (Dry treatment). For biological control spray Trichoderma viridae 5 % L @ 1 ml/lit of water.  For chemical control spray Triadimefon 25% WP @ 200 g or Penconazole 10% EC @100ml or Tebuconazole 18.3% SC + Azoxystrobin 11% @ 300 ml by mixing with 200 liters of water.

Today we will discuss about Yellow mosaic disease of Arhar. The virus produces typical mosaic symptoms of green and yellow on the leaf surface. The whitefly acts as the vector of the disease. Yellowing of leaves reduced significantly reduce the plant productivity. Early-stage infection can stunt even kill the plant. To manage the disease, severely infected plants should be uprooted and destroyed far away from the field. Install 8 yellow sticky traps per acre. To manage whiteflies during primary stage of pest infestation spray Neem Based Pesticide 3000 PPM @ 600 ml/acre. To manage whiteflies chemically spray Thiamethoxam @ 40-gram/acre or Acetamiprid @ 50-gram /acre or Dinotefuran 20 % SG @ 80 g/acre or Flonicamid 50 % WG @ 60- gram/acre or Pymetrozine 50% WG @ 120 g/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water.

Let’s discuss about the Bacterial leaf blight disease management of Arhar. This disease affects all aerial part parts and at any stage of plant growth. At initial stage small water-soaked grow spot appear in leaf which may be irregular in shape and surrounded by golden yellow halo, in advance stage this spot enlarges, and centre tissue become dry and brown created large, blighted area. Leaves may curl, dry out rapidly from tips eventually completely blighted, in stem reddish streaks appear, may sometimes split open release milky oozes. In pod also some streak or blot appears. Infected plant shows stunted growth. Seedlings get blighted due to infection soon after seed germination. To control this disease, treat the seeds with carbendazim/ Mancozeb or @ 2gram/kg seeds and using foliar spray with Streptocyclin (Streptomycin sulphate 90% + Tetracyclin hydrochloride 10%) SP@30 gram+ Copper oxychloride 50% WP @ 500g or (Streptomycin sulphate 9%+ Tetracyclin hydrochloride 1%) + Copper oxychloride 50% WP @ 300gram + 500gram /200 ltr of water per acre.

Today we will discuss about pod bug management in Red gram. Symptoms will appear as when the pods are picked, the bug where the stem and flower buds have sucked the sap, and small pods and flowers fall. Inner pod wall with warty growth or water-soaked spots. Young pods abort and turn dark brown. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM) @ 600-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. After 7-10 days, spray Bacillus thuringenesis @ 400 g/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. Instead of this u can also insert Trichogramma chilonis card at weekly interval after flowering. For chemical control, spray Chlorpyriphos 50 % + Cypermethrin 5 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Profenophos 50 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Emamectin benzoate 5 % SG @ 90 g/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water.

Let’s discuss about the Cercospora leaf spot disease management of Arhar. Round, oval and irregular black-purple spots appear on both sides of the leaves. The leaves gradually turn yellow, that darken to bronze or gray with reddish brown edges, and it appears as shot hole as tissue dies than leaf dries and finally fall. To control this disease remove and destroy the infected plant residues, treat the seeds with Carbendazim @ 2gram/kg seed and spray Carbendazim 50% WP @100 gram or Chlorothalonil75% WP @400 gm or the combined medicines of Carbendazim 12% +Mancozeb 63%WP @400 gram or Azoxystrobin18.2%+Difenoconazole 11.4%W/W SC @200ml or Propiconazole 25% EC @ 200 ml or Hexaconazole @ 300 ml/acre. To spray the fungicide 200-litre of water per acre is required.

Let’s discuss about the Alternaria leaf blight disease management of Arhar. First small circular yellow spots appear on the leaves, and later this colour changes to a dark greyish brown colored spot that appears on the leaves. Finally, these spots coalesce leading to blighting of leaves. Wilting and defoliation of leaves occur at later stage. Sunken spots appear on fruits. Fruits turn yellow and drop prematurely. To control this disease remove and destroy the infected plant residues, treat the seeds with Carbendazim @ 2gram/kg of seed and spray Carbendazim 50% WP @100 gram or Chlorothalonil75% WP @400 gm or the combined medicines of Carbendazim 12% +Mancozeb 63%WP @400 gram or Azoxystrobin18.2%+Difenoconazole 11.4%W/W SC @200ml or Propiconazole 25% EC @ 200 ml/ 200 ltr of water.

The fungus invades the xylem vessels and produces gels that physically block water and nutrient flow. The blockage causes wilting starting with drooping of leaves, petioles and leaflets progressively to the entire plant and then plant collapse. Young seedlings 3-5 weeks collapse, lie flat and have dull green leaves and shrunken stumps. Older plants show yellowing and chlorosis, and wilting often in patches reduce vigour eventually death. Summer cultivation can be done and cover the soil with thin transparent polythene for 45 days. For prevention measures, organic seed treatment can be done with Trichoderma harzinium 1% WP @ 20 gm / kg or Trichoderma viridae 1.5 % WP @ 10 gm / kg of seed or Carboxin + Thiram @ 2gm/ kg of seed before nursery management. In nursery, application of Trichoderma harzianum  1% WP or Trichoderma viridae 1.5 % WP @ 50gm / square meter area before sowing of seeds. Prepare a heap with 2 ton well decomposed cowdung by adding 2 kg of Trichoderma viridae 1.5 % WP or Trichoderma harzianum  1% WP with it. Cover it properly and water it regularly. After 15 days of incubation spread the decomposed material uniformly in the main field. Transplanting should be done minimum 10 days after soil treatment. Drain out excess water from the field. For chemical management, remove the affected plants and drench the soil with Metalaxyl 8%+ Mancozeb 64% WP @ 2gm/liter or Azoxystrobin 11% + Tebuconazole 18.3% SC @ 300 ml/acre or Azoxystrobin 4.8 % + chlorothalonil 40.0 % w/w SC @ 80-100 ml/acre or Azoxystrobin 11% + Tebuconazole 18.3% SC @ 300 ml/acre or Pencycuron 22.9%SC @ 300ml or Carbendazim 50%WP @ 300 gram/acre with 200 liters of water near unaffected plants.

Let’s discuss about the Rust disease management of Arhar. The disease appears as circular reddish-brown pustules/blister which appear more commonly on the underside of the older leaves. Sometimes this pustule are surrounded by yellow halo. When leaves are severely infected, both surfaces are fully covered by rust pustules and shrivelling followed by defoliation resulting in yield losses. To control this disease, treat the seeds with Carbendazim or Mancozeb @2 gram per kg seeds. Dusting with sulphur 85% DP @6-8kg or doing foliar spray with the mixture medicines of Captan 70% +Hexaconazole 5% WP @ 400 gram or Difenoconazole 25% EC @ 200 ml/acre or Tebuconazole 50% + Trifloxystrobin 25% WG @ 80 gram/acre.

Today we will discuss about Tobacco Caterpillar management in Arhar. Freshly hatched larvae are active in the evening and at night, feed gregariously by scraping the chlorophyll content of leaves. soon disperse, hiding usually at the base of plant and near cracks and crevices during the day time. Later stage caterpillar feed voraciously so that only petioles and branches are left behind. Caterpillar bores into the pods and feed on them. Caterpillars also show migration from one plot to another in search of food. To manage this pest during primary stages of pest infestation proper surveillance should be done to collect and destroy egg mass and caterpillar of the pest. Monitor the emergence of adult moths by setting up Pheromone traps having Spodo lure @ 6-8 nos/acre. Check the migration of caterpillars by dusting with Chlorpyriphos 1.5 % D @ 10 kg around the field. For chemical control spray Novaluron 5.25 %+ Indoxacarb 4.25 % @ 400-ml/acre or Lambda Cyhalothrin 4.9 CS @ 300 ml or Flubendiamide 20 % WG @ 120 gram or Chlorantraniliprole 18.5% SC @ 60 ml or Emamectin Benzoate 5% SG @ 80 gram. For spraying of insecticides 200 litre of water per acre is required.

Today we will discuss about management of Leaf webber management in Arhar. When the plant is young, the larvae make silken webs by twisting the leave and live inside the web, initially larvae scrape the leaf surface consuming chlorophyll make the leave skeletonise followed by defoliation. Caterpillar bore into pods and feed the seed inside. In severe cases completely loss of crops happened. To manage at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM) @ 600-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. After 7-10 days, spray Bacillus thuringenesis @ 500 g/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. For chemical control, spray Chlorpyriphos 50% + Cypermethrin 5 % E.C @ 400 ml/acre or Chlorantraniliprole 18.5% SC @ 60 ml/acre or Flubendiamide 39.35% SC @ 60 ml/acre or Lambda cyhalothrin 5 % EC @ 400 ml/acre by mixing it in 200 litre of water. Apply the insecticides in the afternoon hours so that the movement of Honeybees will not be disturbed.

The disease occurs both in young seedlings and grown up plants. The lower leaves show yellowing, drooping and premature defoliation. The discoloured area later turns black and  death of plants occur. The infected plants can be easily pulled out due to the rotting of the roots. Minute dark sclerotia are seen in the collar region and root. To control this disease treat the seeds with Carbendazim @ 2 gram /kg seeds and drench the soil with Validamycin 3% L @ 400 ml or Pencycuron 22.9% SC @ 300ml or Carbendazim 50%WP @ 400 gram/acre. To spray the fungicide 200 liters of water per acre is required.

Let’s discuss about the Phytopthora blight disease management of Arhar. The pathogen affects the crop at any growth stage under favourable environmental conditions. At seedling stage, Infection in seedlings is visible as water-soaked lesions in the leaves, which subsequently become necrotic and the leaflet lesions are circular to irregular spots.  On stems, brown to dark brown lesions distinctly marked from the healthy green portion are formed near ground level. The lesions on the stem and branches increase rapidly, causing the portion of the plant above the lesion to dry out but remain attached to the plant. Wind easily breaks up stems at the infected point. To control this disease treat the seeds with Carbendazim@ 2 gram/ kg seeds. Drench the soil with Metalaxyl 35% WS@ 200 gram or Fosetyl –AL 80% WP@ 400 gram or spray the combination of Metalaxyl M 4%+ Mancozeb 64% WP@ 500 gram/200 ltr of water per acre.

Let’s discuss about the Collar rot disease management of Arhar. The disease appears in the early stages of crop growth, up to 6 weeks from the sowing. Seedlings become chlorotic, joint of stem and root turns soft, slightly contracts and begins to decay. Infected parts turn brown white. Black dots, mustard like sclerotia seen in the  infected plants. To control this disease avoid high moisture at the sowing time and treat the seeds with Carbendzim@ 2gram/ kg seeds. Apply the combination of Metalaxyl-M 4%+ Mancozeb 64%WP@ 2 gram or Carbendazim 50% WP @ 2 gram or Validamycin 3% L@2ml/ltr of water through basal drenching.

Maggots cause damage by boring into the soft seeds and feed on grains.  Dry pods showing pin head size hole. The damaged seeds are unfit for consumption as well as for germination. The extent of damage may be even upto 60-70% during severe infestation. Spray with Emamectin benzoate 5%SG @ 80 gram/acre or Indoxacarb 15.8%SC @ 80 ml/acre or Spinosad 45%SC @ 60 ml/acre by mixing in 200 liters of water.

Arhar flowers and pods are falling off and a larvae is entering the pods and excreting its feaces, destroying them. One of the reasons for the falling off of flowers and pods in Arhar crops is due to the presence of pod borer. The larvae enter the flowers and pods in their young stage and eat them, causing them to fall off. When the pods grow, the larger larvae enter the pods and eat the seeds. Put 10-12 bird perches of bamboo per acre. Go for spraying of neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 3000 PPM) + Shampoo packet (small) @ 30-ml/10 litre of water once pod formation gets started. To manage the pest biologically use Trichogramma chilonis card (150000 eggs/ha from 30 DAS to 2 months of sowing) @ 1/acre at weekly interval for 6 times by splitting each card into 12 pieces at the given perforations. Along with this apply Bacillus thuringensis @ 400 gm/acre by mixing with 200 lit. of water. For chemical control apply Emamectin benzoate @ 80 gm/acre or Spinosad @ 60 ml/acre or Flubendiamide @ 80 ml/acre by mixing with 200 lit of water.

After mating, the adult female moth flies about 1 km away from the house at night, pierces and lays eggs on the top of the ripening pods. Similarly, it lays eggs in the seeds of arhar which are about to be harvested, without our knowledge. Apart from this, beetle from the affected pulse seeds in the warehouse or granary in our house fly at night and lay eggs on the unaffected pulses seeds that are stored due to our carelessness. The eggs appear on the seed surface like a small white spot. The larvae that emerge from them do not come out but enter the seeds and eat the food inside them. Therefore, if we grub it and crush these affected seeds or pulses forcefully with our hands, it gets crushed.
To protect crop from beetle attack, when the crop is maturing in the field in the morning, Apply 10-12 kg of Malathion 5% DP or Fenvalerate 0.4% DP per acre. Alternatively, spray 400 ml of Cypermethrin 10% EC or 200 ml of Lambda-cyhalothrin 2.5% EC per acre mixed in 200 liters of water. Although spraying chemical insecticides during maturity is effective, there is a possibility that residues may remain in the food. Therefore, at this time, mixing 3% neem oil with 0.2% liquid or soap powder, or 0.03% Azadirachtin @ 1 liter in 200 liters of water and spraying can control the pests.
After harvesting, if the water content in the pulses seeds is more than 10%, then the pulse beetle attack is more likely. Therefore, if you want to store the seeds, then dry them in the sun for 4-5 days and add ash or crushed ash to them or add 3% begonia leaves, lemon leaves, lemon leaves dried in the shade and store them in a dry place. But if you do not want to store the seeds but want to store them for consumption throughout the year, then dry the seeds in the hot sun for 4-5 days and then add 25 ml of any edible oil per 10 kg of seeds and rub them with both hands so that a layer of oil falls on each seed. After this, even if you store it throughout the year, the pulses will not be attacked by beetles. Soak the bag in which you will store it by adding 2 ml of Malathion 50% EC per liter of water and leave it for 30 minutes and then dry it. If you are storing it in another container, dry them in the hot sun for 4-5 days and then store so that the stored seeds cannot absorb water vapor from outside on rainy days.

Name of Disease and Insect: Northern Corn Leaf Blight/Turcicum Leaf Blight
Advisory: Oval to elliptical yellowish brown to ashy coloured spots appear on the leaves with yellow halo. The affected leaves dry up and gives a burnt appearance. To control this disease, treat the seeds with Carbendazim or Mancozeb @ 2 grams per kg seeds and by using the foliar spray with Carbendazim 50 %WP @300 grams or Chlorothalonil 75% WP @ 400 gram or the mixture medicines of Carbendazim 12% + Mancozeb 63% WP @ 400 gram per 200 lit of water per acre.

Name of Disease and Insect: Southern Corn Leaf Blight/Maydis Leaf Blight
Advisory: Let's discuss about the management of leaf blight disease in maize cultivation. Small yellowish round or oval spots appear on the leaves. Later these spots enlarge, become elliptical and the centre becomes straw coloured with a reddish-brown margin. Infected leaves show grayish parallel or elongated irregular spots. These spots coalesce and the leaf looks like straw. To control it treat the seeds by adding 2.5 grams of captan or 3 grams of thiram per kg of seeds. To manage this disease spray Mancozeb 75 % WP @ 600-gram/acre or carbendazim 12 % + Mancozeb 63 % WP @ 400-gram/acre or Hexaconazole 5% SC @ 400ml by mixing in 200-litre of water.

Let us discuss about the management of rust disease in Maize. The disease appears as circular reddish-brown spots which appear more commonly on the lower portion of the leaves. When leaves are severely infected, both surfaces are fully covered by rust pustules and shrivelling followed by defoliation resulting in yield losses. To control this disease treat the seeds with Carbendazim or (Carboxin 37.5% + Thiram 37.5% DS)@ 2 gram per kg seeds. Dusting with sulphur 85% DP @ 6-8kg or doing foliar spray with Triadimefon 25% WP @ 50 gram or Chlorothalonil 75% WP @ 400 gram or Difenoconazole 25% EC @ 200ml/200 lit of water per acre.

Small angular yellow to light green spots appear in the upper leaf surface. These lesions enlarge, turn brown can cause the leaf to become papery and die. A greyish yellow or purplish fungal growth develop in lower leaf surface corresponding spots on the top. Leaves may curl; brittle drop off prematurely. Dark brown or black lesion can see on stem and pods which produce small, discoloured, shrivelled seed. The disease can cause plants to be stunted. To manage Downey Mildew disease in mustard crop spray Metalaxyl 8% + Mancozeb 64% W.P @ 400- gram/acre.

Bacterial stalk rot in maize causes water-soaked lesions at the lower stem, foul-smelling ooze, soft rotting of the stalk, and sudden lodging or wilting, usually from tasseling to grain filling. Management involves avoiding waterlogging, improving drainage, using balanced fertilizers, and selecting resistant hybrids. Early infections can be checked by drenching the stem base with bleaching powder (10 kg/ha) through irrigation or copper oxychloride @ 3 g/L. Removing infected plants, maintaining proper spacing, and rotating crops help prevent recurrence.

Charcoal rot appears mainly during drought or high temperatures, causing premature yellowing, wilting, and shredding of the lower stem. Splitting the stalk reveals tiny black microsclerotia giving a charcoal-like look, and the stem becomes dry, brittle, and prone to lodging. Manage charcoal rot by avoiding moisture stress through timely irrigation during tasseling–grain filling and using balanced fertilization with adequate potash. Follow crop rotation, avoid dense planting, and remove infected residues. Treat seed with carbendazim or thiram @ 2–3 g/kg seed and apply Trichoderma harzianum @ 2 kg/acre (mixed with 50 kg FYM) in high-risk fields to suppress early infection.

Elongated dark, thickened lesions develop on the cotyledons of young seedlings. The affected leaves bend downwards. Streaks develop in the tissue of leaves, leaf sheaths and bulbs, exposing black powdery mass of fungus. Seed treatment with Thiram or Carboxin 37.5% + Thiram 37.5% DS @ 2 g/kg seed.

The virus produces typical mosaic symptoms of green and yellow on the leaf surface. The whitefly acts as the vector of the disease. Yellowing of leaves reduced significantly reduce the plant productivity. Early-stage infection can stunt even kill the plant. To manage the disease, severely infected plants should be uprooted and destroyed far away from the field. Install 8 yellow sticky traps per acre. To manage whiteflies during primary stage of pest infestation spray Neem Based Pesticide 3000 PPM @ 600 ml/acre. To manage whiteflies chemically spray Thiamethoxam @ 40-gram/acre or Acetamiprid @ 50-gram /acre or Dinotefuran 20 % SG @ 80 g/acre or Flonicamid 50 % WG @ 60- gram/acre or Pymetrozine 50% WG @ 120 g/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water.

Let's discuss about the management of stem borer in maize cultivation.the middle leaf first dries out and comes out when it is pulled out. When the plant grows, the maize leaves appear to have holes in rows. To control it spray  Cartap hydrochloride 50% SP @ 200 gm/acre  or profenofos 50 EC @ 400 ml/acre or indoxacarb 14.5 s. c @ 500 ml/acre in 200 liters of water or apply carbofuran 3G active ingredient @ 10 kg/acre if small spots appear on the leaves.

During the time when corn is fruiting, caterpillar or larva attacks it and eats the kernels inside. To protect the corn from the attack of the worm, apply Bacillus thuringiensis @ 500 grams per acre or Beauveria bassiana @ 500/ acre and spray 2-3 times in the evening at an interval of 7-10 days or spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM) @ 600-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. To manage the pest chemically, spray Emamectin Benzoate 5 % S.G @ 80-gram/acre or Spinetoram 11.7% S.C @ 100-ml/acre or Chlorantraniliprole 18.5% S.C @ 80-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water.

Caterpillar eat the leaves and damage the shoots. Numerous holes and cut marks are seen on the corn leaves and they also eat the corncompletely. They also leave greenish sticky excrement on the leaves. To manage the pest spray biopesticide Beauveria bassiana @ 1000-gram/acre by mixing in 200- litre of water. Instead of this you can use Trichogramma chilonis card @ 2/acre (100000/ha) at weekly interval for 6 times by splitting each card into 12 pieces at the scheduled points. 10-12 bird perches per acre and remove them before tasselling stage. Install light trap @1-2 / acre. Dust mixture of sand, soil, and wooden ash inside the leaf whorl so that the caterpillars cannot feed on the leaves. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM) @ 600-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. To manage the pest chemically, spray Emamectin Benzoate 5 % S.G @ 80-gram/acre or Spinetoram 11.7% S.C @ 100-ml/acre or Chlorantraniliprole 18.5% S.C @ 80-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. Thank You.

The maggots bore into the central shoot and cause “dead-heart,” where the central leaf dries while outer leaves remain green. Infested seedlings may show stunted growth, withered central whorl, and in severe cases the plant may produce weak tillers that do not form cobs. Management includes timely sowing, maintaining optimum plant population, and keeping fields free of grassy weeds. Seed treatment with imidacloprid 600 FS @ 5–8 ml/kg seed or thiamethoxam 30 FS @ 10 ml/kg seed protects seedlings. For foliar control, spray imidacloprid 17.8 SL @ 3 ml/10 L or lambda-cyhalothrin 5 EC @ 4 ml/10 L at early seedling stage.

During the time when corn is fruiting, caterpillar or larva attacks it and eats the kernels inside. To protect the corn from the attack of the worm, apply Bacillus thuringiensis @ 500 grams per acre or Beauveria bassiana @ 500/ acre and spray 2-3 times in the evening at an interval of 7-10 days. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM) @ 600-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. To manage the pest chemically, spray Emamectin Benzoate 5 % S.G @ 80-gram/acre or Spinetoram 11.7% S.C @ 100-ml/acre or Chlorantraniliprole 18.5% S.C @ 80-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water.

Lets discuss about management of Aphid in maize. Leaves, inflorescence, stalk and young panicle are covered with dark coloured aphids. Honey dew secretion with black ant movements is seen. They suck saps from leaves and stem. yellowing of leaves and stem is also seen. To control this pest spray Thiamethoxam@ 80gram/acre or Acetamiprid@ 50gram /acre or Imidacloprid@6oml/acre mixing it with 200 litres of water.

Nymphs suck the sap and because of continuous sucking of the sap from the leaves, the leaves turn yellow, curled and fall off and the plant becomes weak. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. To manage chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25 % WG @ 50-gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20 % SP @ 50-gram/acre or Fenobucarb @ 400-ml/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Harvesting should be done minimum 1 week after insecticide spraying.

Today we will discuss about stem rot management in maize. visual symptoms like water soaked, brownish discoloured stalks near the ground which emit fowl smell and easily break. infected plants might also exhibit wilting and lodging and unfilled grains.white fungal growth symptom also seen. For prevention seed treatment should be done. Spray hexaconazole 400 ml in 200 litre of water per acre on infected plants.

Today we will discuss about grasshopper management in maize.Symptoms appear as irregular feeding on seedlings and leaf blade,cutting of stem and damage of ear and completely defoliate the plants leaving only the mid ribs. sometimes dead heart appear on plants. To control this insect spray Chlorpyriphos 20 EC @ 500 ml/acre or dust Chlorpyriphos 1.5 DP @10 kg/acre.

Let's discuss about the management of termite in maize crop. Symptoms appears as, Termites bores into the roots and stem due to which the plant soon dries. For its management seed treatment with chlorpyriphos 4ml/kg of seeds. To control termites apply chlorpyriphos dust 10kg/acre.

Today we will discuss about hairy caterpillar management in maize.Young larvae feed gregariously on chlorophyll due to which the leaves look like brownish-yellow in colour.In severely affected plant leaves give an appearance of net or web.To control this pest spray Flubendiamide 20% WG @ 100-120 gm/acre or Indoxacarb 14.5% SC@130 ml/acre or Cypermethrin 4% EC + Profenophos 40%EC @ 400 ml /acre by mixing in 200 liter of water. Other than that use light traps for monitoring.

It damages the crop by feeding underground plant roots causing wilting yellowing of leaves, stunted growth. The grubs chew the fine roots and tunnel into base of the stem. Affected plants can be pulled out effortlessly. Infestation often appears irregular dead or dying in patches. The early symptom of attack in field that plant shows wilt in daytime and recovers at evening. Infestation of this pest leads to wilting of plant by cutting the roots beneath the soil. To prevent this pest, apply Chlorpyriphos dust @ 10-12 kg/acre or Chlorantraniliprole 0.4% GR @ 5 kg at last ploughing or apply this dust above seeds after sowing and then cover it by soil. Collect and kill the adults by inserting light trap @ 4-5/acre during evening 7 to 10. Adults can be collected from nearest host plant like ber, neem, jamun and killed by using insecticide. FYM pit should not be dug near the field. Go for deep summer ploughing to destroy the pupae of this pest. Regular monitoring of the pest should be done. If white grubs are seen in the field, then soil drenching should be done with Chlorpyriphos @ 5 ml/lit of water or Imidachloprid 40% + Fipronil 40% WG @ 170 – 200 gram/lit of water. Instead of this you can also apply Chlorantraniliprole 0.4% GR @ 5 kg/acre in soil.

The plants dried in patches. It bore into root and hollow out the main tap root causing plant death. Damaged plant can be easily pulled from ground. Termite feed on outer layer of pods creating a corcky and rough structure due to scarification. It also bore holes in pod and damage the kernel. Damage often appears in patches particularly in sandy or light soil. To control this spray Bifenthrin @ 400 ml/acre or Fipronil @ 200 ml/acre or Chlorpyriphos @ 400 ml/acre in 200 liters of water.

Nymphs suck the sap from leaves and leaflet tips turn pale yellow due to toxin infected by the nymphs. The tip of leaf shows a v shaped yellowing termed as Hopper burn. The initial symptom of jassid infestation is whitening of veins. After yellowing of leaf the affected leaves begin to curl downward and become necrotic in a typical v shaped giving entire crop a scorched and burnt appearance. The affected leaves become dry and brittle. Eventually the leaves dry up and shed plants over all growth retarded and stunted. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. To manage Jassids chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25 % WG @ 50-gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20 % SP @ 50-gram/acre or Flonicamid 50% WG @ 60 ml/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Harvesting should be done minimum 1 week after insecticide spraying.

It sucks the sap from the stems, leaves, tips and pod, causing young leaves to become twisted and curl inward or downward then the plant to become weak, the leaves to turn yellow, growth stunted and the deformed seeds. Aphids excrete honeydew sooty mould growth in leaves affect photosynthesis. It acts as vectors transmitting viruses’ diseases like mosaic. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @ 600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. Release 5000 no. of eggs of Chrysoperla carnea per acre by mixing with saw dust. Try to conserve other parasitoids in field like Coccinellid beetle and syrphid fly. To manage Aphids chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 60 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Dinotefuran 20 % SG @ 80 g/acre or Flonicamid 50 % WG @ 60- gram/acre or Pymetrozine 50% WG @ 120 g/acre by mixing in 200 litres of water.

Very small, tiny insects suck the sap from leaves, flowers and stems, due to its continuous sucking the silvery streaks or patches on leaves. The leaves turn yellow become papery, crinkled distorted rolled cupped inward especially from tip. The growing point damaged and leaves curl. Thrips transmitted the peanut bud necrosis virus causing severe bud necrosis. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @ 600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Blue sticky traps per acre. At primary stage of infestation, spray Verticillium lecani @ 5 g/lit of water. Along with this release 5000 no. of eggs of Chrysoperla carnea per acre by mixing with saw dust. Try to conserve other parasitoids in field like Coccinellid beetle and syrphid fly. To manage Thrips chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 60 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Dinotefuran 20 % SG @ 80 g/acre or Flonicamid 50 % WG @ 60- gram/acre or Pymetrozine 50% WG @ 120 g/acre by mixing in 200 litres of water.

Freshly hatched larvae feed gregariously by scraping the chlorophyll content of leaves. Grown up larvae feed the entire leaf leaving the petiole alone hiding usually in the soil around the base of the plants during the day as the pest is nocturnal in habit. To manage this pest during primary stages of pest infestation proper surveillance should be done to collect and destroy egg mass and caterpillar of the pest. Faecal pellets are seen in the leaves and on ground which is the indicator of the pest incidence. Monitor the emergence of adult moths by setting up Pheromone traps having Spodo lure @ 8 nos/acre. To manage the pest biologically use Trichogramma chilonis card (50,000 eggs/ha) @ 2/acre at weekly interval for 5 times by splitting each card into 12 pieces at the given preferences. For chemical control spray Emamectin benzoate 5 % SG @ 90 g/acre or Chlorantraniliprole 18.5 % SC @ 80-ml/acre or Novaluron 5.25 %+ Indoxacarb 4.25 % @ 400-ml/acre or Lambda Cyhalothrin 4.9 CS @ 300 ml or Flubendiamide 20 % WG @ 120 gram. For spraying of insecticides 200 litre of water per acre is required.

Young larvae feed on the leaves consuming the chlorophyll and leaving behind the veins, i.e skeletonising the entire leaf causing severe defoliation. Grown up larvae move to buds, flowers and pods making circular holes to feed on the grains inside often inserting only their head and upper body. Larvae destroy developing and mature seeds within the pod, making them hollow and unusual. The key indicator of pest attack is feed holes on leaves and pods. Avoid very close plant spacing. Install bird perches. Install Pheromone trap with Heli lure @ 6-8/acre.  To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 3000 PPM) @ 600-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. After 7-10 days, spray Bacillus thuringenesis @ 400 g/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. For chemical control, spray Chlorpyriphos 50 % + Cypermethrin 5 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Profenophos 50 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Emamectin benzoate 5 % SG @ 90 g/acre or Chlorantraniliprole 18.5 % SC @80-ml/acre or Indoxacarb 14.5 % SC @ 200 ml/acre or Novaluron 5.25 %+ Indoxacarb 4.25 % @ 400-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water.

Young larvae tunnel into the tender leaf tissue creating characteristics whitish or brownish blister like trails or mines on upper epidermis. Older larvae web leaflets together feeding on internal tissue causing them to fold, dry and turn brown. Affected leaves become distorted and crinkled and ultimately dry giving entire crop scorched or burnt appearance from a distance. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM) @ 600-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. Use yellow sticky trap @ 8/acre. Install Light trap@1-2 per acre. You can also use Trichogramma chilonis card @ (1-2)/acre (100000/ha) at weekly interval for 6 times by splitting each card into 12 pieces at the scheduled points. To manage these pests chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25 % WG @ 60-gram /acre or Emamectin Benzoate 5 % SG @ 80- gram/acre or Spinosad 45 % SC @ 75 ml/acre or Chlorpyriphos 50% EC + Cypermethrin 5% EC @ 400 ml/acre or Profenophos 20% EC @ 400 ml/acre or Chlorantraniliprole 18.5% SC @ 80 ml/acre or Spinetoram 11.7 % SC @ 200 ml/acre by mixing in 200 litre of water.

The neonate larvae feed together on the underside of the leaf by scraping the green tissue of the leaves like papery later the larvae defoliate the plant and then eat entire plant, causing the plant to appear stunted. Mature larvae devour the leaves, flowers, mainstem and leaving behind a bare stalk. Caterpillar moves in a large group from field to field making the field like grazed cattle. Catch and monitor the butterfly by setting up a light traps @ 2/acre. Use Tricho cards (50,000- 100000 eggs) @ 2/acre. Spray Chlorpyriphos 50% + Cypermethrin 5 % E.C @ 400 ml/acre or Profenophos 50% EC @ 400 ml/acre by mixing it in 200 litre of water. Red Hairy Caterpillar are nocturnal so insecticide spray should be done at evening hours. Collect the insects by putting Datura, Amari and castor leaves and spray any above-mentioned insecticide. Dusting of Chlorpyriphos 1.5% DP@ 10-kg/acre should be done across the bund to check the infestation from affected field.

The most prominent damage is formation of numerous dark spots on the leaves. The spot reduces the leaf’s ability to performs photosynthesis. Early leaf spots are larger irregular reddish brown on upper surface surrounded by a distinct yellow halo. Late leaf spot is small circular, dark brown on both surface and usually lack a yellow halo. As infection develops and spot coalesces the affected leaf turn yellow, wilt and drop prematurely. The loss of foliage weakens the plant which directly affects the development of pods and nuts. To manage this disease seed treatment should be done with Pseudomonas flouroscence 1.5% AS @10 gm/kg or Carboxin 37.5% + Thiram 37.5% DS @3 gm/kg of seed. To control chemically spray Chlorothalonil 75% WP @ 400-gram/ acre or Mancozeb 75% WP @ 500- gram/acre or Hexaconazole 5% E.C @ 300-ml/acre or Tebuconazole 50% + Trifloxystrobin 25% @ 70 gram/acre by mixing in 200 liters of water.

Collar rot disease causes rotting of seeds and collar region of young emerging seedlings resulting in the collapse of seedlings. Seed rot in soil and fail to germinate, infected seeds appear black with soft watery interval tissue. Young seedlings will suddenly wilt and die often 7-15 days of emergence. The stem area at or just below the soil live or collar region become discolourd light brown to blackish, soft and rotten. The affected plants easily uprooted. In older plants large lesions develop on the stem below the soil surface spreading upward along the branches causing leaves to drop, wilt and eventually entire plant collapse and die. To prevent Collar rot disease in groundnut, treat the seeds with Carbendazim 50% WP @ 2-gram/ per kg of seeds or Tebuconazole 2% Ds @ 1 gram/ kg of seeds or Carboxin 37.5 % + Thiram 37.5% D.S @ 2-gram/ kg of seeds. Apply Trichoderma viridae @ 10-kg/acre or Trichoderma asperellum @10 kg/acre and Neem or Castor Cake @ 200-kg/acre during final land preparation. To manage this disease soil drenching with Carbendazim 50% WP @ 400-gram/acre or Thiophanate Methyal 70 % WP @ 400-gram/acre or Difenoconazole 25 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Carbendazim 12% + Mancozeb 63% WP @ 300 gram/acre or Metalaxyl 8% + Mancozeb 64% WP @ 400 gram/acre by mixing in 200 liters of water.

The first disease symptom is the sudden drying of a branch which is completely or partially in contact with the soil. The leaves turn yellowish to brown and dry but remain attached to the plant. Near soil on leaves and stems white growth of fungus mycelium appeared. To manage stem rot drench soil with Validamycin 3% L @ 400-ml/acre or Captan 70 % + Hexaconazole 5 % WP @ 300-gram/acre.

Let’s discuss about the Rust disease management of Groundnut. The disease appears as circular reddish-brown pustules/blister which appear more commonly on the underside of the older leaves. Sometimes this pustule are surrounded by yellow halo. When leaves are severely infected, both surfaces are fully covered by rust pustules and shrivelling followed by defoliation resulting in yield losses. To control this disease, treat the seeds with Carbendazim or Mancozeb @2 gram per kg seeds. Dusting with sulphur 85% DP @6-8kg or doing foliar spray with the mixture medicines of Captan 70% +Hexaconazole 5% WP @ 400 gram or Difenoconazole 25% EC @ 200 ml/acre or Tebuconazole 50% + Trifloxystrobin 25% WG @ 80 gram/acre.

Due to the Alternaria disease incidence, first small dark greyish brown coloured spots appear on the leaves. Finally, these spots coalesce leading to blighting of leaves. Wilting and defoliation of leaves occurs at later stage. Apart from cabbage and cauliflower the disease incidence can also be observed in broccoli and mustard crop. To manage this disease in Cole crops spray Mancozeb 75 % WP @ 600-gram/acre or carbendazim 12 % + Mancozeb 63 % WP @ 400-gram/acre or Hexaconazole 5% SC @ 400ml   by mixing in 200-litre of water.

Today we will discuss about management of Root rot in Groundnut crop. When the soil is wet, the tip of the plant rots, the roots get rot just near the soil, and finally the plant dies completely. If plant survives leaves may turn yellow and start dropping followed by stunting growth. The entire plant can wilt and die. To manage root rot disease, drenching of root zone and soil should be done with Hexaconazole 5% SC @ 400 ml/acre or Carbendazim 12% + Mancozeb 63% WP @ 200 g/acre or Tebuconazole 50% + Trifloxystrobin 25% WG @ 100 gram /acre. To spray the fungicide 200-litre of water per acre is required.

Today we will discuss about Bud Necrosis disease management in Groundnut crop. Bud necrosis disease is a viral disease transmitted by thrips in groundnut crop. Due to incidence of the disease the newly emerging leaves become small, round, or pinched inwards and rugose with varying patterns of mottling and minute ring spots. Plant becomes stunted with short internodes and short auxiliary shoots. In advanced conditions, the necrosis of buds occurs. Top bud is killed and necrosis spreads downwards. Drastic reduction in flowering and seeds produced are abnormally small and wrinkled with the dark black lesions on the testa. To manage this disease first remove and destroy the infected plants. To manage the carrier of the disease (Thrips) during primary stage of pest infestation spray Neem Based Pesticide (Azadirachtin) 1500 PPM @ 600 ml/acre. To manage Thrips chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 40 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Imidacloprid 17.8% SL @ 60-ml/acre by mixing in 200 litres of water. Thank You.

Today we will discuss about Yellow mosaic disease of Greengram. The virus produces typical mosaic symptoms of green and yellow on the leaf surface. The whitefly acts as the vector of the disease. To manage the disease, severely infected plants should be uprooted and destroyed far away from the field. Install 8 yellow sticky traps per acre. To manage whiteflies during primary stage of pest infestation spray Neem Based Pesticide 3000 PPM @ 600 ml/acre. To manage whiteflies chemically spray Thiamethoxam @ 40-gram/acre or Acetamiprid @ 50-gram /acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. Thank You.

Today we will discuss about leaf spot management in Greengram crop. Round, oval and irregular black-purple spots appear on both sides of the leaves. The leaves gradually turn yellow, dry up, and finally fall off. To manage spray Mancozeb 63%+ Carbendazim 12% WP @ 600-gram/acre or Hexaconazole @ 300 ml/acre. To spray the fungicide 200-litre of water per acre is required.

Today we’ll discuss about the Powdery mildew management of Greengram. Due to this disease, small patches of white powder coating initially develop on both surfaces of older leaves. The superficial growth ultimately covers the entire leaf area. The diseased areas turn brown and dry leading to premature defoliation and death. Fruits remain underdeveloped and are deformed. The disease also creates forced maturity of the infected plants which results in heavy yield losses. Treat the seeds with Carbendazim 50%WP @ 2 gram/ kg of seeds (Dry treatment). For biological control spray Trichoderma viridae 5 % L @ 1 ml/lit of water.  For chemical control spray Triadimefon 25% WP @ 200 g or Penconazole 10% EC @100ml or Karathane (Dinocap 48% EC) by mixing with 200 liters of water for 1 acre area.

Let’s discuss about the Anthracnose disease management of Greengram. The symptom can be observed in all aerial parts of the plants and at any stage of crop growth. Small angular brown lesions appear on leaves, mostly adjacent to veins, which later become greyish white centre with dark brown or reddish margin. The lesions may be seen on the petioles and stem. Minute water-soaked lesion appears on the pods initially and becomes brown and enlarges to form circular, spot with dark centre with bright red or yellow margin. The infected pods have discoloured seeds. To control this disease, treat the seeds with Carbendazim or Mancozeb@ 2 gram per kg seeds and using foliar spray with Carbendazim 12%+Mancozeb 63% WP @400 gram or Copper Oxychloride 50% WG @600gram or Tebuconazole 25.9% EC @ 300 gram or Kasugamycin 3% SL @300 gram /200 lit of water per acre.

Today we will discuss about management of Root rot in Greengram crop. When the soil is wet, the tip of the plant rots, the roots get rot just near the soil, and finally the plant dies completely. To manage root rot disease, drenching of root zone and soil should be done with Validamycin 3 % L @ 400-ml/acre. To spray the fungicide 200-litre of water per acre is required.

Let’s discuss about the Rust disease management of Greengram. The disease appears as circular reddish-brown spots which appear more commonly on the lower portion of the leaves. When leaves are severely infected, both surfaces are fully covered by rust pustules and shrivelling followed by defoliation resulting in yield losses. To control this disease, treat the seeds with Carbendazim or Mancozeb @2 gram per kg seeds. Dusting with sulphur 85% DP @6-8kg or doing foliar spray with the mixture medicines of Captan 70% +Hexaconazole 5% WP @ 0.6 gram or Difenoconazole 25% EC @0.5 ml/lit per acre.

Let’s discuss about the Bacterial leaf blight disease management of Greengram. The fungus attacks all aerial part parts and at any stage of plant growth. Symptoms are circular, black, sunken spots with dark center and bright red orange margins on leaves and pods. In severe infections, the affected parts wither off. Seedlings get blighted due to infection soon after seed germination. To control this disease, treat the seeds with carbendazim/ Mancozeb or @ 2gram/kg seeds and using foliar spray with Streptocyclin (Streptomycin sulphate 90% + Tetracyclin hydrochloride 10%) SP@30 gram+ Copper oxychloride 50% WP @ 500g or (Streptomycin sulphate 9%+ Tetracyclin hydrochloride 1%) + Copper oxychloride 50% WP @ 300gram + 500gram /200 ltr of water per acre.

Today we will discuss about Aphid management in Greengram. It sucks the sap from the stems, leaves, tips and pod, causing the plant to become weak, the leaves to turn yellow and the deformed seeds. This insect is a carrier of the mosaic viral disease. As a result, the leaves to turn yellow and curl. Flowering is delayed. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @ 600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. At primary stage of infestation, spray Verticillium lecani @ 5 g/lit of water. Along with this broadcast 5000 no. of eggs of Chrysoperla carnea per acre by mixing with saw dust. Try to conserve other parasitoids in field like Coccinellid beetle and syrphid fly. To manage Aphids chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 60 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre by mixing in 200 litres of water. Thank You.

Today we will discuss about Whitefly management in Greengram. Because of sucking the sap from the leaves, the leaves turn yellow, curl and dry up and the plant becomes weak. It is a carrier of yellow vein mosaic disease. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @ 600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. At primary stage of infestation, spray Verticillium lecani @ 5 g/lit of water. Along with this broadcast 5000 no. of eggs of Chrysoperla carnea per acre by mixing with saw dust. Try to conserve other parasitoids in field like Coccinellid beetle and syrphid fly. To manage Whitefly chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 60 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre by mixing in 200 litres of water. Thank You.

Today we will discuss about Thrips management in Greengram. Very small, tiny insects suck the sap from leaves, flowers and stems, causing the leaves to curl. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @ 600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Blue sticky traps per acre. At primary stage of infestation, spray Verticillium lecani @ 5 g/lit of water. Along with this broadcast 5000 no. of eggs of Chrysoperla carnea per acre by mixing with saw dust. Try to conserve other parasitoids in field like Coccinellid beetle and syrphid fly. To manage Thrips chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 60 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre by mixing in 200 litres of water. Thank You.

Today we will discuss about pod borer management in Greengram. The larvae of this insect first feed on the young leaves, then eat the growing pods or fruits by piercing the seeds. When eating the seeds, the insect head stays inside pod and half of its body is seen hanging outside.  To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 3000 PPM) @ 600-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. After 7-10 days, spray Bacillus thuringenesis @ 400 g/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. For chemical control, spray Chlorpyriphos 50 % + Cypermethrin 5 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Profenophos 50 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Emamectin benzoate 5 % SG @ 90 g/acre or Chlorantraniliprole 18.5 % SC @80-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. Thank You.

Today we will discuss about Tobacco Caterpillar management in Greengram. Freshly hatched larvae are active in the evening and at night, feed gregariously by scraping the chlorophyll content of leaves. soon disperse, hiding usually in the soil around the base of the plants during the day. Later stage caterpillar feed voraciously so that only petioles and branches are left behind. Caterpillar bores into the groundnut pods and feed on them. Caterpillars also show migration from one plot to another in search of food. To manage this pest during primary stages of pest infestation proper surveillance should be done to collect and destroy egg mass and caterpillar of the pest. Monitor the emergence of adult moths by setting up Pheromone traps having Spodo lure @ 6-8 nos/acre. Check the migration of caterpillars by dusting with Chlorpyriphos 1.5 % D @ 10 kg around the field. For chemical control spray Novaluron 5.25 %+ Indoxacarb 4.25 % @ 400-ml/acre or Lambda Cyhalothrin 4.9 CS @ 300 ml or Flubendiamide 20 % WG  @ 120 gram. For spraying of insecticides 200 litre of water per acre is required. Thank You.

The adult fly punctures tender leaves and stems for egg-laying. Maggots bore inside the stem, damaging internal tissues and blocking nutrient flow, leading to wilting or plant death. spray Chlorpyriphos 50 % + Cypermethrin 5 % E.C @ 200-ml/acre or Lambda Cyhalothrin 5% EC @ 300 ml/acre or Profenophos + Cypermethrin @ 200 ml/acre or Novaluron 10% EC @ 200 ml/acre by mixing with 200 litres of water.

The maggots bore into developing pods and feed on maturing seeds, causing significant yield and quality losses. Infestation is more severe during humid and cloudy weather. Remove and destroy affected pods during field inspection. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM) @ 600-ml/acre by mixing in 200-litre of water. You can also use Trichogramma chilonis card @ (1-2)/acre (100000/ha) at weekly interval for 6 times by splitting each card into 12 pieces at the scheduled points. For chemical control, spray Chlorpyriphos 50 % + Cypermethrin 5 % E.C @ 200-ml/acre or Lambda Cyhalothrin 5% EC @ 300 ml/acre or Profenophos + Cypermethrin @ 200 ml/acre or Novaluron 10% EC @ 200 ml/acre during afternoon hours by mixing with 200 litres of water.

Today we will discuss about Jassid management in Greengram. Nymphs suck the sap and because of continuous sucking of the sap from the leaves, the leaves turn yellow, curled and fall off and the plant becomes weak. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. To manage Jassids chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25 % WG @ 50-gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20 % SP @ 50-gram/acre or Fenobucarb @ 400-ml/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Harvesting should be done minimum 1 week after insecticide spraying. Thank You.

Today we will discuss about the management of Bihar hairy caterpillar in Greengram cultivation. The caterpillars attack the young growing leaves, shoots in early stage and later they eat entire plant, causing the plant to appear stunted. Catch and kill the Bihar hairy caterpillar's butterfly by setting up a light traps @ 2/acre. Use Tricho cards (50,000- 100000 eggs) @ 2/acre. When there is dew in the morning dusting with Chloropyriphos 1.5% dust @ 10 kg per acre or spray with Profenophos @ 400 ml/acre or Profenophos + Cypermethrin @ 200 ml or Emamectin benzoate @ 80 g/acre in 200 liters of water.

Let’s discuss about the Wilt disease management of Greengram. The crop may be affected by disease at any stage. Dead seedlings or matured plants, typically in patches, are the field symptoms of wilt. At the seedlings stage, after 3-5 weeks of sowing. Whole seedlings collapse and lying flat and on the ground with dull green leaves and a shrunken stem petioles and leaflets drop off at the adult stage, followed by the entire plants.  To control this disease deep summer ploughing should be done, always use disease free seeds and avoid sowing when temperatures are high. Treat the seeds with Tebuconazole 2% DS@ 2gram/kg of seed and drench the soil with Metiram 75% WG@ 300 gram with 200 ltr of water per acre.

Nymphs stay under the leaves and suck the sap and because of continuous sucking of the sap from the leaves, causing the leaf edges to turn red and the leaves to curl upwards and fall off and the plant becomes weak. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. To manage Jassids chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25 % WG @ 50-gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20 % SP @ 50-gram/acre or Fenobucarb @ 400-ml/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Harvesting should be done minimum 1 week after insecticide spraying.

Nymphs and adult suck the sap from the stems, leaves, tips and pod, causing young leaves to become twisted and curl inward or downward then the plant to become weak, the leaves to turn yellow, growth stunted and the deformed seeds. Aphids excrete honeydew sooty mould growth in leaves affect photosynthesis. It acts as vectors transmitting viruses’ diseases like mosaic. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @ 600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. Broadcast 5000 no. of eggs of Chrysoperla carnea per acre by mixing with saw dust. Try to conserve other parasitoids in field like Coccinellid beetle and syrphid fly. To manage Aphids chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 60 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Dinotefuran 20 % SG @ 80 g/acre or Flonicamid 50 % WG @ 60- gram/acre by mixing in 200 litres of water.

The nymps and adult suck sap from tender leaves, from under surface due to continuous sucking leaves turn yellow, curl inward. Feeding reduce plant vitality, leading to yellowing, drooping and premature leaf drop. Heavy infestation leads to stunted growth of plant. Whitefly secrets honeydew, which gives rise to sooty mould growth on leaf surfaces, it will reduce photosynthesis. It transmits viral disease like YVMV so it is very harmful as it is act as a vector of virus disease. Trimming of field bunds and cleaning for weed removal should be done. Split application of nitrogenous fertilizer should be followed. To manage these pests at early stage of infestation spray neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 1500 PPM @ 600-ml/acre by mixing it in 200-litre of water. Install 8-10 Nos of Yellow sticky traps per acre. At primary stage of infestation, spray Verticillium lecani @ 5 g/lit of water. Along with this broadcast 5000 no. of eggs of Chrysoperla carnea per acre by mixing with saw dust. Try to conserve other parasitoids in field like Coccinellid beetle and syrphid fly. To manage Whitefly chemically spray Thiamethoxam 25% W.G @ 60 gram/acre or Acetamiprid 20% S.P. @ 50 gram/acre or Tolfenpyrad 15 % EC @ 400-ml/acre or Dinotefuran 20 % SG @ 80 g/acre or Flonicamid 50 % WG @ 60- gram/acre or Pymetrozine 50% WG @ 120 g/acre or Thiamethoxam 8.8% + Chlorantraniliprole 17.5% SC @ 50 ml by mixing in 200 litres of water.

Both nymphs and adults suck sap from stems and leaves. In the growing parts of the leaves and on shoots, the pest congregates in large numbers and forms a thick coating, releasing honeydew in the form of excretion, which gets covered with black sooty mold, weakening the affected plant and turning it black, thereby reducing its fruit-bearing capacity. For management of it clean the land by cleaning the parts of previous crops, uncultivated grass, wood, shoes. Leave Chrysoperla carnea grubs @ 2 nos per tree. Apply neem cake while applying the paste. After 15 days of transplanting, spray with 5% neem seed kernel extract once every week. Put 2 handfuls of rice in a plastic bottle, fill it half with water and squeeze it tightly. Keep it in shade and open and close the lid in the morning and evening. After doing this for 5-7 days, if it is sprinkled on mealy bug, the upper part of the pest will die and after that, if the approved insecticide is applied, it will be controlled well. Chemically apply Fipronil 8% SC @ 400 ml. or acetamiprid 20% SP @30 grams or Imidacloprid 70% WG@ 14 grams or Difenthiuron 50% WP@ 240 gm or Thiamethoxam 12.6% + Lambda cyahalothin 9.5% ZC@ 300 ml in 200 liters of water per acre and sprinkle.

Cotton flowers and pods are falling off and a larva is entering the bolls and excreting its feaces, destroying them. One of the reasons for the falling off of flowers and bolls in cotton crops is due to the presence of American bollworm. The larvae of these moths enter the flowers and bolls in their young stage and eat them, causing them to fall off. When the bolls grow, the larger larvae of these moths enter the bolls, squares and eat the fibers and they defecate inside and destroy the beetles. The larvae of the American bollworm only feed on the bolls by inserting their heads inside from above. To manage this pest, place one or two pheromone traps per hectare in the field. This will help you to know when the presence of this pest is in the field. Release Trichogramma egg-laying parasitic insect per acre in the field five times at an interval of 7-10 days from the beginning of bollworm. To control this pest use pheromone trap @ 10/acre. Put 10-12 bird perches of bamboo per acre. Go for spraying of neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 3000 PPM) + Shampoo packet (small) @ 30-ml/10 litre of water once boll formation get started. To manage the pest biologically use Trichogramma chilonis card (150000 eggs/ha from 30 DAS to 2 months of sowing) @ 1/acre at weekly interval for 6 times by splitting each card into 12 pieces at the given perforations. Along with this apply Bacillus thuringensis @ 400 gm/acre by mixing with 200 lit. of water. For chemical control apply Emamectin benzoate @ 80 gm/acre or Spinosad 60 ml/acre or Flubendiamide @ 80 ml/acre by mixing with 200 lit of water.

Cotton flower buds are drying up and falling off and when the buds are opened, it looks like two seeds have been joined or double seed formation and there is a pink-colored larvae seen inside the joined seeds. If you see such symptoms, then you can know that it is an attack of pink cotton bollworm. The larvae of this pest appear towards the end of the cotton crop, enter the flower buds and destroy them and then stay inside the boll and connect the two adjacent young seeds and stay inside it. At that stage, the cotton is harvested and the pest enters the next crop through the seeds. To manage this pest, place one or two pheromone traps per hectare in the field. This will help you to know when the presence of this pest is in the field. Release Trichogramma egg-laying parasitic insect per acre in the field five times at an interval of 7-10 days from the beginning of bollworm. To control this pest use pheromone trap @ 10/acre. Put 10-12 bird perches of bamboo per acre. Go for spraying of neem-based pesticide (Neem Oil 3000 PPM) + Shampoo packet (small) @ 30-ml/10 litre of water once boll formation get started. To manage the pest biologically use Trichogramma chilonis card (150000 eggs/ha from 30 DAS to 2 months of sowing) @ 1/acre at weekly interval for 6 times by splitting each card into 12 pieces at the given perforations. Along with this apply Bacillus thuringensis @ 400 gm/acre by mixing with 200 lit. of water. For chemical control apply Emamectin benzoate @ 80 gm/acre or Spinosad 60 ml/acre or Flubendiamide @ 80 ml/acre by mixing with 200 lit of water.

Cotton flowers and pods are falling off and a larvae is entering the bolls and excreting its feaces, destroying them. One of the reasons for the falling off of flowers and pods in cotton crops is due to the presence of bollworms such as the Striped bollworm and the American bollworm. The larvae of these moths enter the flowers and pods in their young stage and eat them, causing them to fall off.
